      "sourceBlockId": "203-3A-203-3a-teach-check-2",
      "title": "General Circuit Principles",
      "role": "explanation",
      "stage": "teach_check",
      "progressionRule": "feedback_deeper",
      "nextStepId": "203-3a-203-3a-teach-check-3",
      "objective": "Explain how circuits originate and terminate within a distribution system.",
      "completionMode": "respond",
      "retrievalText": "Every electrical circuit has a clear start and end point. The 'origin' of a circuit is the Distribution Board (DB) or, in a house, the Consumer Unit (CU). This central hub is where the main supply is split into smaller, manageable branches. Each branch is protected by its own fuse or circuit breaker.\nFrom the DB, the circuit travels through the building's wiring system to reach its 'termination' point. This termination is the final outlet, such as a socket-outlet or a light fitting. It is vital that circuits remain distinct; they must not be interconnected randomly. By keeping each circuit separate from the origin to the load, we ensure that the protective device can accurately monitor the current and 'trip' (cut the power) if a fault occurs on that specific path.",
      "basicQuestions": [
        {
          "questionText": "What is the technical name for the central hub where a domestic circuit originates?",
          "answerGuidance": [
            "Ring Final Circuit"
          ]
        },
        {
          "questionText": "Where does a circuit technically end or terminate?",
          "answerGuidance": [
            "Final outlet",
            "Socket-outlet",
            "Light fitting"
          ]
        },
        {
          "questionText": "Why must circuits remain distinct and not be interconnected randomly?",
          "answerGuidance": [
            "Accurate monitoring by protective devices",
            "Ensure the breaker trips correctly",
            "Isolate faults to one path"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "deeperQuestionText": "Why is it necessary for every circuit to have its own dedicated protective device at the distribution board rather than sharing one?",
      "deeperAnswerGuidance": [
        "Allows for specific current ratings per circuit; Prevents a single fault from losing all power; Enables the device to detect overcurrent on a specific cable size; Ensures safe isolation for maintenance"
      ],
      "hint": "Where does the electricity first enter the individual circuit's protective device?"
    },
    {
      "id": "203-3a-203-3a-teach-check-3",
      "sourceBlockId": "203-3A-203-3a-teach-check-3",
      "title": "Ring and Radial Circuits",
      "role": "explanation",
      "stage": "teach_check",
      "progressionRule": "feedback_deeper",
      "nextStepId": "203-3a-203-3a-teach-check-4",
      "objective": "Distinguish between ring final circuits and radial circuits and explain how each operates.",
      "completionMode": "respond",
      "retrievalText": "There are two main ways to wire power outlets: Radials and Rings. A Radial circuit is like a single branch on a tree. It starts at the DB, goes to the first socket, then the second, and stops at the very last outlet. If the cable breaks anywhere, everything 'downstream' loses power.\nA Ring Final Circuit is a continuous loop. It starts at the DB, visits every socket in the room, and then returns to the exact same terminal in the DB. This creates a circle. Because it is a loop, electricity can travel in two directions to reach any single socket. This 'load sharing' means the circuit can safely handle more power than a radial circuit of the same cable size. If there is one break in a ring, the sockets will usually still work because the current can travel around the other side of the loop, though this is a fault that must be repaired.",
      "basicQuestions": [
        {
          "questionText": "Which circuit type runs from the distribution board and stops at the final outlet without returning?",
          "answerGuidance": [
            "Radial Circuit"
          ]
        },
        {
          "questionText": "In a ring final circuit, where does the cable return to after visiting every socket?",
          "answerGuidance": [
            "Distribution Board",
            "Consumer Unit",
            "The same terminal"
          ]
        },
        {
          "questionText": "What operational advantage does a loop configuration provide regarding the electrical load?",
          "answerGuidance": [
            "Load sharing",
            "Current travels in two directions"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "deeperQuestionText": "If a ring final circuit suffers a single break in the loop, why is this considered a dangerous fault even if the sockets still appear to work?",
      "deeperAnswerGuidance": [
        "The circuit effectively becomes two radials",
        "Load sharing is lost",
        "One part of the cable may become overloaded",
        "The protective device may not protect the now-undersized path"
      ],
      "hint": "Which circuit type forms a complete circle back to the fuse box?"
    },
    {
      "id": "203-3a-203-3a-teach-check-4",
      "sourceBlockId": "203-3A-203-3a-teach-check-4",
      "title": "Control, Data, and Alarm Circuits",
      "role": "explanation",
      "stage": "teach_check",
      "progressionRule": "feedback_deeper",
      "nextStepId": "203-3a-203-3a-teach-check-5",
      "objective": "Identify the purpose and operational characteristics of control, data, and alarm circuits.",
      "completionMode": "respond",
      "retrievalText": "Not every circuit is meant to deliver high power. Control circuits act as the 'brain' of a system; they carry small signals to tell a larger 'muscle' circuit when to turn on, such as a thermostat controlling a central heating pump. Data circuits carry high-speed information for computers and internet connections.\nAlarm circuits monitor sensors for fire or security. These systems often operate at Extra Low Voltage (ELV), which is typically below 50V AC. Because these signals are sensitive, they must be kept separate from high-voltage power cables. If they are too close, the 'electrical noise' from the power cables can interfere with the data, causing errors or false alarms. We use specialized cables, sometimes with metal shielding, to protect these signals from interference.",
      "basicQuestions": [
        {
          "questionText": "What is the purpose of a control circuit in an electrical system?",
          "answerGuidance": [
            "Manage the operation of another circuit",
            "Act as the brain of the system",
            "Signal a larger circuit to turn on"
          ]
        },
        {
          "questionText": "What voltage category is typically used for data and alarm circuits to keep them below 50V AC?",
          "answerGuidance": [
            "Extra Low Voltage",
            "ELV"
          ]
        },
        {
          "questionText": "What is the result of 'electrical noise' from power cables affecting data cables?",
          "answerGuidance": [
            "Interference",
            "Data errors",
            "False alarms"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "deeperQuestionText": "What is the main reason data and alarm cables be physically separated or shielded from standard power cables within an installation?",
      "deeperAnswerGuidance": [
        "Prevents electromagnetic interference; Protects sensitive low-voltage signals; Ensures data integrity and system reliability; Avoids induced voltages from high-current cables"
      ],
      "hint": "Why do we keep internet cables away from heavy power cables?"
    },
    {
      "id": "203-3a-203-3a-teach-check-5",
      "sourceBlockId": "203-3A-203-3a-teach-check-5",
      "title": "Wiring Systems by Environment",
      "role": "explanation",
      "stage": "teach_check",
      "progressionRule": "feedback_deeper",
      "nextStepId": "203-3a-203-3a-worked-example-1",
      "objective": "Select suitable wiring systems for domestic, commercial, industrial, and hazardous environments.",
      "completionMode": "respond",
      "retrievalText": "We choose wiring systems based on the 'mechanical protection' they need. In domestic homes, we use flat PVC cables hidden in walls. In commercial offices, we use trunking—rectangular plastic or metal boxes with removable lids—which makes it easy to add or change cables later. Industrial sites use Steel Wire Armoured (SWA) cables or heavy-duty metal conduit (pipes) to protect wires from being crushed by machinery.\nIn 'Hazardous Environments' like petrol stations, where flammable vapors are present, we use specialized sealed systems to prevent any internal spark from causing an explosion. For emergency systems like fire alarms, we use Mineral Insulated Copper Clad (MICC) cable, often called 'pyro.' MICC uses compressed mineral powder instead of plastic insulation, allowing it to survive extreme heat and keep the power running during a fire.",
      "basicQuestions": [

          "answerGuidance": [

          ]

      ],
      "deeperQuestionText": "Why is MICC cable selected for fire alarm systems in public buildings rather than standard thermoplastic (PVC) cables?",
      "deeperAnswerGuidance": [

      "hint": "Which cable uses mineral powder to survive fire, and which uses steel wires for physical strength?"
    },
    {

      "completionMode": "respond",
      "retrievalText": "Scenario: You are tasked with installing power for a row of four workbenches in a commercial vehicle repair workshop. The mechanics will use various power tools, and there is a high risk of cables being struck by heavy equipment or tools.\nStep 1: Identify the environment and mechanical risks. Because this is a commercial workshop with a high risk of impact, standard domestic-style cables (flat PVC) are unsuitable. You need a system that provides 'mechanical protection.'\nStep 2: Choose the circuit type. Since there are multiple outlets that will be used simultaneously by different workers, a Ring Final Circuit is the best choice. This allows for load sharing across the loop, providing more capacity for the power tools.\nStep 3: Select the wiring system. To protect the conductors from being crushed or cut, you select galvanized steel conduit. parsed.title: Circuit Types and Wiring Systems
parsed.subject: C&G 2365
parsed.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To enable learners to describe the operational principles of various circuit types and select appropriate wiring systems for specific installation environments.
steps.retrievalText:
Lesson: Circuit Types and Wiring Systems
To enable learners to describe the operational principles of various circuit types and select appropriate wiring systems for specific installation environments.
Why this matters: These are the most common circuits encountered; understanding their basic function is the foundation of installation work.
Core chunk map: Lighting and Power Circuits; General Circuit Principles; Ring and Radial Circuits; Control, Data, and Alarm Circuits.
By the end of this lesson you should be able to explain the main idea accurately and use it in one practical case.
We will work in short chunks: teaching first, then checks, then example, practice, and a final integrative task.

steps.title: Lighting and Power Circuits
steps.objective: Describe the basic operational principles of lighting and power/heating circuits.
steps.retrievalText:
In electrical installations, we divide the system into different 'final circuits' to ensure that a fault in one area doesn't shut down the whole building. Lighting circuits are designed for low-power loads, typically using 1.0mm² or 1.5mm² cables protected by a 6 Amp (6A) circuit breaker.
Power and heating circuits are built for much heavier loads, such as electric heaters or water cylinders. Because these appliances draw more current, they require thicker conductors and higher-rated protective devices to prevent the cables from overheating. Every circuit's primary job is to deliver energy from the supply to the 'load' (the appliance or light) safely. While they share the same goal, the cable size and the fuse rating are matched specifically to what the circuit is powering.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the typical circuit breaker rating used for a standard lighting circuit?
Why do power and heating circuits require thicker conductors than lighting circuits?
What is the primary purpose of every final circuit in an installation?
answerGuidance: Deliver energy safely to the load

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way matching the cable size and protective device to the specific load type contribute to the overall safety of the building?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Prevents conductors from overheating under load; Ensures the fuse or breaker trips during a fault; Reduces the risk of electrical fires; Maintains the integrity of the wiring system
steps.hint: Think about why a heater needs a thicker cable than a single LED light bulb.

steps.title: General Circuit Principles
steps.objective: Explain how circuits originate and terminate within a distribution system.
steps.retrievalText:
Every electrical circuit has a clear start and end point. The 'origin' of a circuit is the Distribution Board (DB) or, in a house, the Consumer Unit (CU). This central hub is where the main supply is split into smaller, manageable branches. Each branch is protected by its own fuse or circuit breaker.
From the DB, the circuit travels through the building's wiring system to reach its 'termination' point. This termination is the final outlet, such as a socket-outlet or a light fitting. It is vital that circuits remain distinct; they must not be interconnected randomly. By keeping each circuit separate from the origin to the load, we ensure that the protective device can accurately monitor the current and 'trip' (cut the power) if a fault occurs on that specific path.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the technical name for the central hub where a domestic circuit originates?
Where does a circuit technically end or terminate?
Why must circuits remain distinct and not be interconnected randomly?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is it necessary for every circuit to have its own dedicated protective device at the distribution board rather than sharing one?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Allows for specific current ratings per circuit; Prevents a single fault from losing all power; Enables the device to detect overcurrent on a specific cable size; Ensures safe isolation for maintenance
steps.hint: Where does the electricity first enter the individual circuit's protective device?

steps.title: Ring and Radial Circuits
steps.objective: Distinguish between ring final circuits and radial circuits and explain how each operates.
steps.retrievalText:
There are two main ways to wire power outlets: Radials and Rings. A Radial circuit is like a single branch on a tree. It starts at the DB, goes to the first socket, then the second, and stops at the very last outlet. If the cable breaks anywhere, everything 'downstream' loses power.
A Ring Final Circuit is a continuous loop. It starts at the DB, visits every socket in the room, and then returns to the exact same terminal in the DB. This creates a circle. Because it is a loop, electricity can travel in two directions to reach any single socket. This 'load sharing' means the circuit can safely handle more power than a radial circuit of the same cable size. If there is one break in a ring, the sockets will usually still work because the current can travel around the other side of the loop, though this is a fault that must be repaired.

basicQuestions.questionText: Which circuit type runs from the distribution board and stops at the final outlet without returning?
In a ring final circuit, where does the cable return to after visiting every socket?
What operational advantage does a loop configuration provide regarding the electrical load?

steps.deeperQuestionText: If a ring final circuit suffers a single break in the loop, why is this considered a dangerous fault even if the sockets still appear to work?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The circuit effectively becomes two radials
Load sharing is lost
One part of the cable may become overloaded
The protective device may not protect the now-undersized path
steps.hint: Which circuit type forms a complete circle back to the fuse box?

steps.title: Control, Data, and Alarm Circuits
steps.objective: Identify the purpose and operational characteristics of control, data, and alarm circuits.
steps.retrievalText:
Not every circuit is meant to deliver high power. Control circuits act as the 'brain' of a system; they carry small signals to tell a larger 'muscle' circuit when to turn on, such as a thermostat controlling a central heating pump. Data circuits carry high-speed information for computers and internet connections.
Alarm circuits monitor sensors for fire or security. These systems often operate at Extra Low Voltage (ELV), which is typically below 50V AC. Because these signals are sensitive, they must be kept separate from high-voltage power cables. If they are too close, the 'electrical noise' from the power cables can interfere with the data, causing errors or false alarms. We use specialized cables, sometimes with metal shielding, to protect these signals from interference.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the purpose of a control circuit in an electrical system?
answerGuidance: Manage the operation of another circuit
Act as the brain of the system
Signal a larger circuit to turn on

basicQuestions.questionText: What voltage category is typically used for data and alarm circuits to keep them below 50V AC?
What is the result of 'electrical noise' from power cables affecting data cables?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main reason data and alarm cables be physically separated or shielded from standard power cables within an installation?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Prevents electromagnetic interference; Protects sensitive low-voltage signals; Ensures data integrity and system reliability; Avoids induced voltages from high-current cables
steps.hint: Why do we keep internet cables away from heavy power cables?

steps.title: Wiring Systems by Environment
steps.objective: Select suitable wiring systems for domestic, commercial, industrial, and hazardous environments.
steps.retrievalText:
We choose wiring systems based on the 'mechanical protection' they need. In domestic homes, we use flat PVC cables hidden in walls. In commercial offices, we use trunking—rectangular plastic or metal boxes with removable lids—which makes it easy to add or change cables later. Industrial sites use Steel Wire Armoured (SWA) cables or heavy-duty metal conduit (pipes) to protect wires from being crushed by machinery.
In 'Hazardous Environments' like petrol stations, where flammable vapors are present, we use specialized sealed systems to prevent any internal spark from causing an explosion. For emergency systems like fire alarms, we use Mineral Insulated Copper Clad (MICC) cable, often called 'pyro.' MICC uses compressed mineral powder instead of plastic insulation, allowing it to survive extreme heat and keep the power running during a fire.

basicQuestions.questionText: Which wiring system consists of a rectangular enclosure with a removable lid for easy cable access?
What type of cable is specifically designed with a steel layer for mechanical protection in industrial environments?
What material is used as insulation in MICC cable to allow it to survive extreme heat?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is MICC cable selected for fire alarm systems in public buildings rather than standard thermoplastic (PVC) cables?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It is fireproof and does not melt; Maintains circuit integrity during a fire; Ensures emergency systems continue to operate; Mineral insulation survives temperatures that destroy plastic
steps.hint: Which cable uses mineral powder to survive fire, and which uses steel wires for physical strength?

steps.title: Worked Example: Selecting a Wiring System for a Commercial Workshop
steps.objective: Demonstrate the reasoning process for selecting a circuit type and wiring system based on a specific environment and load requirement.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are tasked with installing power for a row of four workbenches in a commercial vehicle repair workshop. The mechanics will use various power tools, and there is a high risk of cables being struck by heavy equipment or tools.
Step 1: Identify the environment and mechanical risks. Because this is a commercial workshop with a high risk of impact, standard domestic-style cables (flat PVC) are unsuitable. You need a system that provides 'mechanical protection.'
Step 2: Choose the circuit type. Since there are multiple outlets that will be used simultaneously by different workers, a Ring Final Circuit is the best choice. This allows for load sharing across the loop, providing more capacity for the power tools.
Step 3: Select the wiring system. To protect the conductors from being crushed or cut, you select galvanized steel conduit. This is a solid metal tube that can withstand the rough environment of a garage.
Step 4: Determine the origin and termination. The circuit will originate at the Distribution Board (DB), run through the conduit to each socket-outlet on the workbenches, and then the final leg of the cable will return to the same terminal in the DB to complete the ring.
Final Result: A 32A Ring Final Circuit installed using single-core thermoplastic cables drawn into a galvanized steel conduit system.
Takeaway: Always select your wiring system based on the physical environment (mechanical protection) and your circuit type based on how the load will be distributed (radial vs. ring).
steps.hint: Think about whether the environment is 'tough' enough to need metal protection and whether a loop or a single line is better for multiple users.

steps.title: Guided Practice
steps.objective: Select a circuit type and wiring system for a commercial educational environment by mirroring the worked example logic.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are installing power for a school science laboratory. There are 12 socket-outlets arranged around the perimeter walls for student use.
Step 1: Identify the environment. This is a commercial/educational setting. While not as 'rough' as a garage, the cables still need protection from students and cleaning equipment.
Step 2: Choose the circuit type. Because many students will use power at the same time, you need a circuit that can handle the shared load across all sockets.
Step 3: Select the wiring system. You need a system that is neat but provides mechanical protection and allows for future additions. PVC trunking with a removable lid is the standard choice here.
Step 4: Origin and termination. The circuit starts at the distribution board, loops through every socket, and returns to the same breaker.
steps.questionText: Based on the scenario, which circuit type is best suited for the 12 sockets to allow for shared load capacity?
steps.hint: Think about the circuit that 'loops' back to the board for shared loads.

steps.title: Practice
steps.objective: Independently determine the correct circuit type and wiring system for an industrial application.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: An industrial warehouse requires a power supply for a single, high-capacity 3-phase extraction fan mounted on a wall. The cable must run along a route where forklifts operate, posing a high risk of crushing or impact damage.
Consider the nature of the load (single point of use) and the level of mechanical protection required for an industrial environment with heavy machinery.
steps.questionText: For a single high-capacity fan in an industrial warehouse, which circuit type is most appropriate?
steps.hint: Since it is a single load, it doesn't need to loop back. For the wiring, think of the 'armoured' cable or metal tube used to resist being crushed by a forklift.

{
  "terms": [
    {
      "term": "Ring Final Circuit",
      "simpleDefinition": "A power circuit that starts at the consumer unit, loops through every socket, and returns to the same protective device.",
      "anchor": "A continuous loop that starts and ends at the same fuse or breaker.",
      "misconception": "Thinking it is just two radial circuits joined together; it must be a complete, unbroken loop to safely share the electrical load."
    },
    {
      "term": "Radial Circuit",
      "simpleDefinition": "A circuit that runs from the consumer unit to the points of use and ends at the final outlet without returning.",
      "anchor": "A single line that stops at the last point, like a branch on a tree.",
      "misconception": "Believing radials are only for lighting; they are also used for high-power single appliances like cookers or showers."
    },
    {
      "term": "SWA (Steel Wire Armoured)",
      "simpleDefinition": "A heavy-duty cable with a layer of steel wires for mechanical protection, used in industrial or underground settings.",
      "anchor": "The 'armoured' cable used for tough environments where cables might get hit or crushed.",
      "misconception": "Thinking the steel wires are only for protection; they are often used as the circuit's earth (CPC) as well."
    },
    {
      "term": "MICC (Mineral Insulated Copper Clad)",
      "simpleDefinition": "A fireproof cable with copper conductors inside a copper sheath, packed with compressed mineral powder.",
      "anchor": "The ultimate fireproof cable, often called 'pyro', used for emergency systems.",
      "misconception": "Thinking it is a standard flexible cable; it is rigid and can survive extreme heat that would melt thermoplastic cables."

      "misconception": "Thinking conduit is just for aesthetics; its primary job is providing mechanical protection to the wires inside."

issues.whyItMatters: Automated marking systems struggle to parse multiple distinct concepts bundled into one string, leading to false negatives or marking ambiguity for the learner.
issues.suggestion: Split each distinct point into its own string within the answerGuidance array.

issues.problem: The question asks for a 'circuit type' (Radial), but the hint directs the learner toward a 'wiring system' (SWA/Conduit).
issues.whyItMatters: This creates cognitive dissonance for beginners who are just learning to distinguish between the electrical configuration (circuit type) and the physical containment (wiring system).
issues.suggestion: Align the hint to focus on the single-point-of-use nature of the fan to prompt 'Radial'.

issues.suggestion: Group these into primary and secondary acceptable answers or require the technical term 'current draw'.

issues.excerpt: "answerGuidance": [ "Ring Final Circuit" ]
issues.problem: The answer guidance is factually incorrect for the question asked.
issues.whyItMatters: The question asks for the name of the central hub (Consumer Unit/Distribution Board), but the guidance provides a circuit type (Ring Final Circuit). This will cause the system to mark correct learner answers as wrong.
issues.suggestion: Change the answer guidance to ["Consumer Unit", "CU", "Distribution Board", "DB"].

issues.excerpt: "answerGuidance": [ "MICC uses compressed mineral powder instead of plastic" ]
issues.problem: The answer guidance contains a descriptive sentence instead of the specific term requested.
issues.whyItMatters: The question asks to 'identify the single best wiring system'. A learner who correctly answers 'MICC' or 'Pyro' may be marked down because the guidance expects a full explanatory sentence.
issues.suggestion: Change the answer guidance to ["MICC", "Mineral Insulated Copper Clad", "Pyro"] and move the explanation to the feedback.

issues.excerpt: The 'origin' of a circuit is the Distribution Board (DB) or, in a house, the Consumer Unit (CU).
issues.problem: While the teaching text is clear, the subsequent check (Question 1) contradicts it via the answer guidance.
issues.whyItMatters: Beginners rely on consistency. Teaching that the hub is a CU but then requiring 'Ring Final Circuit' as the answer for the hub's name creates fundamental confusion about electrical terminology.
issues.suggestion: Ensure the answer guidance strictly matches the terminology introduced in the retrieval text.

strengths: Clear outcomes and a helpful 'Why this matters' section that sets the professional context.
Excellent use of specific technical values (6A, 1.0mm²) to ground the theory in practice.
The explanation of circuit distinction for the sake of protective devices is very well written.
issues: The first basic question has incorrect answer guidance (Ring Final Circuit instead of Consumer Unit).
suggestedFixes: Update the answer guidance to match the 'Consumer Unit' or 'Distribution Board' terms used in the text.

strengths: The 'tree branch' vs 'loop' analogy is perfect for beginners.
Good emphasis on the 'brain' vs 'muscle' distinction and the importance of ELV separation.
Clear differentiation between trunking and conduit based on the removable lid.

issues: Answer guidance is a descriptive sentence rather than the specific term requested.
suggestedFixes: Simplify answer guidance to the cable name (MICC) to ensure robust marking.
